commentBody: Not bad for a Saturday. Finished pretty quick!
And the “Horse walks into a bar” jokes start in 3…2…1…

Pretty easy for a Saturday imo. 

Side note, I’ve decided “DRS” vs “MDS” is among my least favorite “could be either” answers in crosswords.
That felt like a typical Saturday during the solve, but was over sooner than I expected. Hadn't thought of CRASH BLOSSOMS for a long while but was happy to be reminded. of it.

So glad to learn there’s an actual term CRASH BLOSSOMS!
“Milk Drinkers Turn to Powder”
“Drunk Gets Nine Months in Violin Case”
“Dead Man Noted Among Realtors”

I was proceeding briskly until I ground to a screeching halt in the bottom four rows. Spam instead of BILL, tax instead of TAB, fine instead of FAIR, not knowing OLGA Tokarczuk, and a couple of improvidently placed typos took some time to sort out.  Also took me longer than it should have to figure out NESTSIN.

Learning the term CRASHBLOSSOM alone made this puzzle SOFUN.
40 down is incorrect.  Should end in a "k."  Stumped me until I figured out that it got past the eds.

This is a comment on Saturday's Midi puzzle. Isn't there a mistake in the answer for "Fancy eaters," 3 Down's clue? Or maybe it's supposed to be tongue in cheek rather than pretty much the opposite meaning of the answer?

Very intriguing grid and filling it so cleanly is NO MEAN FEAT. TIL CRASHBLOSSOMS, that was fun, and I appreciated the “remote locations” clue. The bottom half of the grid was a bit crunchy and I did enjoy the time I spent on this puzzle. I smiled imagining birds on telephone lines phoning home. 
BUT I shouldn’t be able to fill in just about Every. Down. Entry. in the top half without having to think.  With all due respect to Caitlin, I disagree that the clue for SIGHTS is particularly tricky, once you get past a moment’s ambiguity. Certainly the clues for THY, HATS, SALUD, THEFT, LOAM and ONCE aren’t. To be very specific,”the Count de la Fere” may be a Saturday clue but “Alexander Dumas’…” shouldn’t be. I get it, not everyone has the Three Musketeers memorized (I saw “Slum Dog Millionaire”) but if you don’t, adding Dumas doesn’t help you—and if you do, it just makes it a gimme.
That’s my two cents, anyway, for what it’s worth. And for those who say just shut up and enjoy the puzzle, I did, and said so, but that doesn’t mean I can’t give my opinion about how to make it more Saturday-worthy.

Excellent puzzle!  A good mix of fun and challenging.

Thank goodness HEADS OR TAILS didn’t fit for [Flip remark?]. 
I had enough trouble with that section. 

I’ll add to the CRASH BLOSSOM fun ….

Farmer Bill Dies in House
Jerk Injures Neck, Wins Award
Eye Drops Off Shelves
I never heard of “digital shorts” or “crash blossoms,” and got sidetracked by “okay” and “fine,” for a double Natick, but intuited it out eventually..

I got stuck thinking the clue for 45 down was trying to be clever, and maybe “Down Under band” didn’t necessarily mean a band from Australia. Google informed me that  ABBA in 1976 had an iconic TV special titled “Down Under” filmed during a tour of Australia, and I spent approximately forever trying to make that work. Anyone else fall for this?
That southwest corner was a bear. Without DIGITALSHORTS (thanks Lonely Island!) I wouldn't have stood a chance.
So much fun with all the novel entries! Over too fast though.

When I ask my chatty car salesman friend where he works, he says a lot.

(He's always calling me on his sell phone.)
Enjoyed it!

Merriam-Webster has an interesting piece on crash blossoms: - https://www.merriam-webster.com/wordplay/crash-blossom-words-were-watching A quote from the entry: A headline blares "Whale watching boat carrying 27 sinks," and you wonder: Why is the whale watching a boat, and how can you tell that it's watching the boat? And why was the boat carrying 27 sinks? A missing hyphen can give rise to a lot of questions!

The entire upper half was superbly done and CRASH BLOSSOMS was a nice TIL moment. BILL instead of SPAM was a fun misdirect too.

But CRASH BLOSSOMS crossed with OLGA, PULIS and LILITH was way too Naticky for me and ended up being a super frustrating solving experience.

I’ve enjoyed the puzzles this week while away on our spring break. I liked seeing ATHOS, ETHOS and OATHS in the same grid. On our drive today, the Talking Heads song Once in a Lifetime popped up on our playlist twice which includes the line, “My God, WHAT HAVE I DONE?” https://youtu.be/5IsSpAOD6K8?si=8WdCn9TUj94iQDx1 This has been a challenging week personally as our daughter got her wisdom teeth removed a couple of days before our trip started and ended up at the ER on Monday night. Doing much better, but wow, I’m exhausted. I need a vacation after my vacation!!

The bottom section was unsolvable for me. You can probably guess my only gimme there. I tried to figure out the other clues for some 20 minutes but then I just gave up, resorting to checks and ultimately reveals. I'm only ashamed of not getting ACDC sooner.

The crossing of NESTS(o/I)N and SAK_ was a natick, for me. Yes, I don't know all the names of all the people in the world. Some hold it against me but somehow I don't think that's fair.
For some reason DIGITALSHORTS made me think of Sponge Bob Square Pants.

Good one! Took a few passes of the grid, several clues needed lots of crossing letters, a few "never heard of that" moments, plenty of replacing wrong first ideas... Exactly how it should be!
Some more fun crash blossoms (from Mental Floss):
"Juvenile Court to Try Shooting Defendant"
"Milk Drinkers Turn to Powder"
"Chester Morrill, 92, Was FED Secretary" (obituary)
"Jacksonville Pornography Free, Officials Say"

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Raced through the top and west/middle muttering under my breath about pandering puzzle editors, etc. etc. and then almost committed seppuku (i.e. did a look up) after 30 minutes on the bottom stack.

Man, that was close!

TIL what a CRASHBLOSSOM is after just hail-Marying it and CALLITINTHEAIR bedeviled me until I realized it could not possibly end in HEADS.

Overall serious slalom despite the top bunny-slope start.

WHEW!

CRASHBLOSSOM made it a Saturday. 

No complaints on difficulty. 

A great puzzle.
I did terribly.  Completely flummoxed in the S and SW.  Needed three lookups to finish.  Never heard of CRASHBLOSSOMS in my 66 yrs.  :-(
Nearly crashed on "crash blossoms" but luckily some of the other clues pulled me through!
Still irritated that the link to wordplay column doesn't open a browser window like it used to, but stays inside the app. Why?

I'd heard of SAKI as an author but wasn't familiar with either of the titles in the clue. "The Open Window" is listed as one of the most anthologized short stories in the English language. Short and worth a read! https://americanliterature.com/author/hh-munro-saki/short-story/the-open-window

As with most commenters, I didn’t know the term CRASH BLOSSOMS before solving today. But it was still intuitive enough (in that it’s comprised of two words, and the ending of BLOSSOMS is pretty unique when you’re a few letters shy) that I found it to be a perfectly Saturday entry. Just enough to guess from crossings, while still learning something new.
Remote locations… LOL

👏👏👏👏 
Oh yes! That was a Saturday! That feeling when you know for a fact that there are heaps of clues you’ll never get and your weekend is ruined, then you slowly chip away and… it ever so slowly comes together. 
The anticipation of entering the last letter and hoping you’ll hear the tune and not the withering ‘almost there..’ message… 
Now I’m in a good mood!

I was feeling quite smug as I raced through the long spanners, then I hit the bottom set with a CRASH. INXS briefly before accepting no long span would begin with X. Settled on AC/DC then spent an inordinate amount of time staring at white space. Sheepdogs? No. Author? No. I don’t watch SNL so that was a blank. As for 55A, blimey. 

So TIL several things, which is good. I can get on with my day with a light heart.

Great puzzle today. I'm thankful that, for no particular reason, I decided that my soundtrack to today's crossword would be Back to Black. A pretty random choice for a Saturday morning - isn't it funny how utterly meaningless coincidences can brighten one's mood!

It didn't stop me getting pretty stuck in the bottom section though after breezing through the rest of it. Luckily my wife happens to know that Mark Zuckerberg owns two PULIS. The rest fell into place after I finally decided to change "spam" to BILL. I mean, don't some people want to receive bills?

I’ll be surpassed if there are “it’s a Tuesday” comments. 
Top three long entries were delightful. 
SW was brutal for me. Given 4/5 downs were proper nouns. 

But I enjoyed the challenge. And gold star never hurts. 

Thank you Kareem.

OLGA Tokarczuk is one of my very favourite writers, so I was delighted to see her featured here. Would recommend everything she’s written to those who haven’t heard of her, but ‘Flights’ (which won the 2018 Booker) and ‘Drive Your Plough Over the Bones of the Dead’ are particular favourites.
Okay, who’s coming here to say it was too easy?! As Bart Simpson might say while making an online comment, you can eat my DIGITALSHORTS

That bottom half was NO MEAN FEAT, but all worth it for the fun of learning about CRASH BLOSSOMS. How have I never heard that term before?

A few more great examples:
“Child’s Stool Great For Use in Garden”
“Eighth Army Push Bottles Up Germans”
“St John Ambulance to Teach Teens to Help Stab Victims”

And then there’s the related, possibly apocryphal, story of Cary Grant receiving a telegram from a fact-checking editor: “HOW OLD CARY GRANT?”  His reply: “OLD CARY GRANT FINE. HOW YOU?” Not a crash blossom, just another example of having fun with ambiguity.

As a relatively recent crossworder I'm not ashamed to say that I wasn't even close to completing that one. 6 correct answers out of a dozen guesses when I gave up (I would have got the playwright if I knew the correct spelling of his surname!).

Kudos to those who managed it.
CRASH BLOSSOMS is now the name of my fantasy girl band.  I eternally regret not having taken a photo of a sign at a motel in DeKalb Illinois many years ago, advertising FREE WOMEN IN BUSINESS SEMINAR

Oh my word, I somehow got through most of it without many hiccups. The top half fell into place rather quickly and I learned CRASH BLOSSOM from a Tom Scott linguistics video. Anyone else watched Tom Scott years ago?
OLGA Tokarczuk. I did this crossword this afternoon during the intervals in a brilliant stage adaptation of Drive Your Plow Over the Bone of Our Dead. (Belvoir St Theatre)

What an odd puzzle for me.  The top half felt like a Monday.  For each of the first three across entries, the phrases were the first things that popped into my head.  A brief check with the downs confirmed them.

Then came the bottom half.  One unknown after another.  Was Adam married to Lilian?  No idea about the dogs, never heard of DIGITAL SHORTS (sounds like something you would put on a naked cartoon character), the wonderful but new to me CRASH BLOSSOMS.  spam instead of BILL and fee for TAB further complicated things.  

The first 7 rows represented 13% of my solve time with the last 8 accounting for 87%. I would have preferred the top half to be more Saturday like, but the bottom more than made up for it.  

It was a LOPsided but ultimately enjoyable solve.

Great Saturday puzzle for me. Also enjoyed seeing all the expressions appear. Favorite clues/answers were "why the long face" and "call it in the air ". My biggest trouble was right upper corner with hemen/sights/teen idol.

A puzzle with so much white space is always going to be daunting at first, and, in fact, it wasn't until the fifth row that I got anything. I was glad to come in about ten minutes below my average.

Plenty of room for misdirection--clAy before LOAM, dIGiTS before SIGHTS, dOg before LOP. For a while I was wondering if there were literary circles in which "For Esme with Love and Squalor" was routinely just called "Esme" until I took a flier with H.H. Munro.

Clever new way to clue the old fencing standard and SOFAS (one of my favorite clues of the week, along with yesterday's [Joey of children's fiction]), and NARCS.

Thanks!

I just wanted to leave a note that this puzzle totally got me. I’m actually weirdly happy about it. I didn’t stop in to get any clues. I will come back to it now and then and I really don’t see how I’m going to get it. I have very little for the long ones on the top and the long ones on the bottom. Kudos to the constructor.

Flew through the top, then came to a screeching halt with two confident own-goals in the lowest tier: INXS (also from Down Under) and SSN (also memorized).  Cost some time to unwind those, but enjoyed the solve.

The symmetrical placement of ETHOS and ATHOS was  NO MEAN FEAT.

Bottom stack alone made this a worthy Saturday.
Top 65% came to me very easy. Bottom not so much but I kept at it and solved without help.
Top  11 lines a breeze. Bottom 4: abject failure.

Good to know that 'Tokarczuk' is the name of a minor planet, as well as a literary star.

OLGA deserves a prize for the title alone of "Drive Your Plow Over the Bones of the Dead".

Oh, superb Saturday!

Areas of whoosh, areas of fierce battle, upper-tier punny clues, and freshness … the kind of gotta-love-it freshness like the aroma of a bakery.

I’m going to focus on that.  Out of the 13 long answers, 10 are NYT debut answers.  Gorgeous answers, such as AVOID AT ALL COSTS, CALL IT IN THE AIR, NO MEAN FEAT, THAT SAYS A LOT, and WHAT HAVE I DONE.  Wow!

A puzzle pulsing with gorgeous freshness has magic to it, like the inventive work of an artist that makes your jaw drop when you see it for the first time.

Sealing the deal were the stellar parallel clues for two of those debut answers: [Rued remark?] for WHAT HAVE I DONE, and [Flip remark?] for CALL IT IN THE AIR.

You dazzled me, Kareem, with your last puzzle (2/12/26), with its brilliant Valentine’s Day theme, and the dazzle continues today.  You have the talent and you put in the work.  Oh yes, more please – and thank you!

Whew. Typical tough Saturday for me, and a lot of pondering and working the crosses. And...  that triple stack on the top almost seems like appropriate theme answers. Oh, and eleven debut answers; don't recall the last time I saw that. Still an impressive feat of construction and overall just a nice challenge. 

And... puzzle finds today I'll put in a reply. 

....

TIL CRASH BLOSSOM, and the headline that led to that neologism.

But as a teacher of informal logic, I have long known the informal fallacy known as "amphiboly", which amounts to much the same thing.

And so I have a lot of amusing examples of this fallacy.  Here are some of my favorites:

PROSTITUTES APPEAL TO POPE

COURT TO TRY SHOOTING DEFENDANT

FARMER BILL DIES IN HOUSE

IRAQI HEAD SEEKS ARMS

PARAMEDICS HELP  DOG BITE VICTIM

Feel free to add your favorites.

I generally detest conversational phrases, but in this puzzle the many easy down clues made them fairly simple to fill in. 

Speed ramped up in the middle; It helped that I knew SAKI, even though that has always been a kea/loa for me, since I don't drink the had stuff. 

But then, like most everyone else, the bottom nearly made me cry uncle. I did know PULIS, but thought OLGA might be Oleg, and knew nothing about Syrian political parties. Haven't watched SNL in decades except for an occasional YouTube clip, and had no idea that ACDC was Aussie. When I finally filled in the unknown CRASH BLOSSOMS and CALL IT IN THE AIR, I was sure there was an error (pen on paper, so no gold star or music or whatever you all get). Then I looked at CALL IT IN THE AIR and the clue again, and the light came on. But that bottom third made this a worthy Saturday puzzle, for once.

"Handcrafted" is my word for today as this was "Such a blast".  For me this was one of those puzzles where it just helps to know stuff, which saved my neck in the bottom stack - although it was a bit of a jolt to learn that the rock band hailed from "Down Under".   Very nicely done and thanks.

Ah, the "dreaded" PULIS. No, I don't mean that you should fear this dog breed. Nor that it's name should be especially hard to remember or to spell. No, I'm referring to it's corded coat that looks like it's covered in dreadlocks. A beautiful breed, if you don't mind spending all your free time grooming your pet. https://www.thesprucepets.com/puli-dog-breed-profile-4774459

Solved without help, but took almost half-an-hour, much of which was spent working out the bottom of the grid. CRASHBLOSSOM was an unknown, as was PULI. SSN before PIN made things more difficult as well. Fortunately I knew COE, BAATH, LILITH and ACDC, and came up with OLGA. 

CLAY rather than LOAM for "Moldable soil" slowed me down up top, as did a delay in recalling ATHOS.

Still a few too many "gimme" clues for a Saturday puzzle, but this one gave me a good workout ... and just of few moments of panic generated by the bottom of the grid.

Nice puzzle!
Hard bottom. In a good sense.

A very fine, very fresh themeless, which, until recently, would have appeared on a Friday. Alas, no Saturday challenge again this week. (No, I did not know every answer from the clue; I never do. This is a crossword; I use the cross words.)

Caught a few 55A decades before they had that name. Some would have been to WEEPOVER.

21D: I will note if not nit that ACELA is one of the services on the [Line]; the [Line] is the Northeast Corridor.

Thanks, Kareem.
 


Thanks, Kareem

There was a lot to like about this puzzle, starting with that beautiful, impressive, unusual grid, that provided for a lot of longer answers.
And the longer answers were all colloquial, none felt forced.  (The only clunker, to my ear, was NESTS IN, but that's nothing to complain about.)
Some beautiful clues, my favorites being "Flip remark?" and "Plug amount".
And the amusing crossing of FAIR and [A]FOUL.

A few too many gimmes for this solver, but I'm not going to complain about the inclusion of OLGA Tokarczuk and Sam SHEPARD.  And the Fitzgerald quote was both amusing and insightful.

So, a big Saturday thanks to Kareem Ayas!
Update: took another look at the top part and it all came together in about four minutes. I’m trying the bottom some more. Still no helps.

For the first time I find I am entirely in agreement with mostly everyone else's comments. Partly because of the lack of pop/sport  trivia, sorry, references. But also great clues, and I didn't mind looking up a few complete unknowns.

I am not sure I'll have any company when I describe my experience with this puzzle...
I started right in the center and just whizzed through the entries, both long and short.  Knew SHEPARD, knew SAKI, knew ACELA, knew COE College, knew PELE, knew LILITH....Pinged on all of the longer phrases down to a certain point...Filled in the SE (slowing down) and then...  I ran AFOUL of the band, the writer, the dog, the 'term', the SNL feature, and ran out of gas.  
TEN letters I could not figure out, all clustered in the corner, sneering at me.
Wow, Kareem.  Ouch. Take no prisoners, eh?
 I hope my old white head looks good up on your trophy wall!
I'm headed off to lick my wounds.
Next week at this time DHubby and I will be packing for our flight to Seattle on Sunday.  Here's hoping that on the following Sunday--the 26th of April--some Puzzlers will see fit to visit Cloud City Coffee in "Maple Leaf"--early afternoon--for a Meet-up.  We can put some (actual) names and faces to our cruciverbal friends.

Update #2: Woohoo! The bottom half crumbled as well. Then I didn’t get the music. Then I went over the whole puzzle across and down and couldn’t find anything. Then I thought maybe NESTSIN and SAKI rather than NESTSoN and SAKo. BINGO! 

For those people who have not been doing this as long as some of us (and there are people who have been doing it a lot longer than I have), this is one of those puzzles that challenges veterans. The thing is, it didn’t get me angry. It was wonderful. Including breaks, it was 48 minutes. And, I used no help whatsoever. If you have to use help, I agree with those who say use it. It’s how you learn. It’s just never the way I learned.

Maybe, for me, nothing beats the thrill of really, really thinking I’m stumped and never going to get it. Then, I solve it. It can actually carryover into life. That may sound weird and so be it.

Thanks to the constructor and Will for their fine job. It was tough and fair. The way I know this is I eventually got it. That’s all that counts. This is one of those occasional puzzles where those of us who “complain“ about how the puzzles are easier get our wish granted of tougher puzzles.

Had an otherwise short, smooth-sailing, and flawless Saturday, when I got too hasty with INXS, and did a reveal - also too hasty! I'll blame it on my wife who is Australian! 

Crash blossom? In all my years, have never come across that term! Still...good to know that I can still learn something new every day.

So, so easy throughout most of it.  I couldn't find any resistance at all.  Then I got to the very bottom and encountered two long answers I never heard of: DIGITAL SHORTS and CRASH BLOSSOM.  I took a wild guess at the first and the second came in.

BAATH reminds me how troubling political events that are momentous at the time fade from the  memory as new and even more disturbing political events arise to crowd them out.  Or maybe that's only true of MY memory!  In any case, BAATH didn't come to me without the two "A"s and you would have thought it was a reference from the Middle Ages.

I was greatly helped in that section by the fact that a close friend of mine had a PULI that I met.  You would not believe the hair of that dog!!  Cathy always emphasized the dog's Hungarian roots.

Why did I think it was pOE college before remembering it's COE?

I've wracked my brain to try and find a reason why an ambiguous news headline would be called a CRASH BLOSSOM.  I've come up empty.

This was stacked and colorful and fun -- but again, too easy for a Saturday.

Another hand raised for the “raced through 90% of the grid, then spent most of my total solve time staring at the last ~10 squares in the bottom-left corner” category.

Btw that’s a pretty brilliant corner, considering that I was able to fill it in without knowing OLGA, PULIS, DIGITALSHORTS, CRASHBLOSSOM, or LILITH (though that last one did spark some familiarity once I got it from crosses).

The combination of guessability and ambiguity for FAIR and AFOUL, along with the inferrability of ACDC (who I would have sworn was an English band until just a few minutes ago), provides a great foundation for the rest of the corner.

Dear Kareem,

Thank you for a really great Saturday puzzle.  Just the right mix of word play, trivia, new and old references and a fresh fill to make it a slow burn and a fun fill. 

👍

Jimbo

Esmé is one of my favorite short stories! I highly recommend Saki. Here's a link to Esmé. It's a quick read, well worth a few minutes of your time. I enjoyed the puzzle, by the way. The bottom part was tough but FAIR (or fine) for a Saturday. https://www.gutenberg.org/files/3688/3688-h/3688-h.htm#esme
After all these years how did I not know they were Ozzies?

Another terrific construction from Kareem, whose puzzles are always so sharp and interesting. The top was much faster than the bottom because there were several answers I just didn’t know in the SW. But I was confident of AFOUL and CALLITINTHEAIR and it came together without much of a fight.

I wish we could retire [Playground retort] as a clue. It feels so bland. And besides, CANSO? Is that really something a ten year old would say? How about a troubadour’s song or a strait of Nova Scotia? Yes, they’re more obscure but inferable.
Oh, no! It’s Mr. Bill
I'm clearly not an expert on Hungarian sheepdogs.

A sunny little SO-FUN Saturday,  hafta hand it freely that there's NO MEAN FEAT to WEEPOVER.

Beautifully colloquial long ones that almost make it easy to remember the Comte de la Fere was one of Dumas' Mousquetaires who HELD EPEES, if not rapiers or sabers. Admitting problems with wanting two Ts in my MASERATIS, as well as a geographic marker along I-95, like the Macon-Dixon line... instead of ACELA

Now comes the bottom & the WEEPOVER, despite a fair start with PULIS (Hungarian), LILITH (Cabala), BAATH (from somewhere that stores AA words) and BILL (don't we all?)... I *knew* ACDC was more likely than ABBA, but *really* wanted FEEL IT IN THE AIR and CAPITOL SHORTS, Also thought TRASH BLOSSOM more appropriate than CRASH BLOSSOM, having never heard of either.

Didn't help that Tolstoy got in my head with his "All happy families" quote, which had me go with HOME where HERO should have rested. So it was a slow rassle with that miserable retort at 41D, and a look-up of the suspected COE University, sadly not on my radar till now.  Finally realizing that 51A was a *coin* flip led the whole gallimaufrey "home"

All in all, enjoyed the hand to hand combat with Kareem A, and will AIR my one working nit in a reply to self

“You’re going to try to scale El Capitan *that* way? Why the *long* face? “

(“Because of all the spring flowers growing at the bottom. You know - the crash blossoms.”)

This puzzle went up and down, in terms of difficulty.
I'm still confused why there are circled letters in this puzzle.  Sage, ilad and tmilk ???
I'd like to thank vampire/witch/daemon books for providing the pathway for LILITH to eventually enter my solve.

When I want a refresher on true First-world problems, I come to the comments and read the complaints of “too easy.”

Three minutes faster than my average but I had fun, learned some things, and was able to forget the crash blossoms of the world for a while.

Thank you!

Caitlin Lovinger,
So glad it’s you🙂

Alas, I can’t seem to make it past a 30-day streak, or thereabouts. And here I was thinking I’m good at crosswords or something. Lower section is brutal. Hats off to those of you who thought it was smooth sailing.

Thank you for this lively, Saturday grid, Kareem -- I can see how its construction would have been NO MEAN FEAT.😊

TIL the term CRASH BLOSSOM and have enjoyed other examples among today's comments! 

I smugly inserted INXS for 45D, but saw pretty quickly that band was going nowhere (with due respect to Michael Hutchence😇).  I also racked my brain for a bit on 25D until the crosses opened it up (not a lot of MASERATIS here in Maine).😂

Honest question: how do you solve puzzles like this if you don't know the trivia?

I didn't know ACDC was Australian (I tried "inxs"). Never heard of OLGA Tokarczuk. Never heard of PULIS. Kept switching between "spam" and "junk" for BILL, and had "amnot" for CANSO.  Never heard of CRASHBLOSSOM nor DIGITALSHORTS. Never heard of COE, and had no idea BAATH was Syrian.

The only two fills I had in the lower left corner were LILITH and HERO. The whole thing really made me feel bad.

Really good puzzle!  Really, really good.

Yes...  ha ha ha...  yes!

That bottom third made this the hardest puzzle--for me-- since I started solving around Xmas. Streak-breaker with three lookups AND an autocheck to correct 3 bad squares.
Not complaining.
I'm old school - I wear analog shorts.
AFTER DOING SATURDAY’S PUZZLE, NYT PUZZLER SAYS BOTTOM LEFT HARD

I loved the clues today.  So many different twists and plays on words. 

Very refreshing
Loved the long entries…always a pleasure to solve those! Great puzzle for an otherwise gloomy day.
Didn't W nickname that creepish Karl Rove "Crash Blossom"? Or was it something a bit more fitting......
Really like this one even though it took me forever to get the bottom half.  Great to learn about crash blossoms!

At 12:31, this is probably my fastest Saturday event (for to a strange glitch, the app has my best at under 3 minutes). The only square that stumped me was the C in PCS/ACELA, as I'm unfamiliar with the train and had "PGS" as an abbreviation for playground. Quite a few other references clocked very quickly.

Great puzzle for me. Took work, and I got through without a look-up. The top half went fast - the bottom, no so much. 
I'm way behind on cleaning, though laundry has moved along, and I walked 5 miles. Beautiful, sunny day on the CT shore.
BTW - Raise your hand if allergies are kicking your hindquarters!
Happy Saturday!
Fun top 2/3s. Absolutely brutal bottom third.
I join the ranks of those who were introduced today to Crash Blossom. Tip of the hat!

At first glance I didn’t make a lot of progress on this one - some of the middle filled in nicely but the entire upper and lower portions were empty.

Once I was able to get the THAT / WHAT / WHY stack out of the way, the rest started to fill in nicely - except for the bottom three across clues.

Of course I remember LASER CATS and other DIGITAL SHORTS but that didn’t help; CRASH BLOSSOM was totally new and CALLITINTHEAIR didn’t fall into place until ATTACHEE became ATTACKER.

After all of that, I tripped over NESTSON / NESTSIN and SALUD / SALUA.

Oh well.

Fantastic puzzle though, challenging and unique.
Anyone else notice that Atlantic puzzles have gotten easier now that Paolo Pasco isn't the creator?

I know the song "Strange Fruit" made famous by Billie Holiday, but didn't retain (if it ever registered) that the TREE was a poplar. What I do recall is that the lyrics were an original poem by a Bronx schoolteacher, Abel Meeropol,  who at one time had James Baldwin as a student. Meeropol was an open leftist, and adopted the two sons of Julius and Ethel Rosenberg when they were executed for espionage early 1950s.

Lilith is not arcana to feminists. Riffing off the discrepancies between the two reports  of creation in Genesis 1 and 2, a whole group of legends about evil uppity Lilith vs good compliant Eve emerged early in Jewish lore. @John and @Andrzej
As to NESTS IN...clued with "calls home"...  A NEST, of course, is NOT a bird's "home."  It's a necessity for the breeding season (if well-hidden, a safe spot to lay one's eggs and then tend one's hatchlings.  After one or more broods, the nest is abandoned.  Some large birds return to a prime nesting site annually.  The chicks of waterfowl and ground-dwelling birds emerge from their shells ready to forage for food in contrast to songbirds, etc., who must be fed and protected until they fledge.  The NEST is a temporary way-station.

I never comment on here but as an owner of a number of Pulik over the last 40 years I take issue with 40 Down.  The plural for a Hungarian sheepdog is not Pulis, it is Pulik.  The same goes for Hungarian livestock guardians, they are not Komondors, the plural is Komondorok.
In English, either plural is acceptable. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/puli

@MaryEllen

I found PULIS online also, but I don't know if I would call them "cute." More like "absolutely amazing looking" or "crash blossoms" or "something from another planet," maybe. I wonder how a person can even take care of a dog like that!! I'd try it!

@Spupilup, true, but they do this all the time in the NYT crosswords. Plural DEER is DEERS, fish is FISHES, and so on. Then there’s a dozen or more Latin/scientific/medical terms that just get an S slapped on for a Crossword plural. Drives me nuts, but it’s a losing battle. 

p.s. The Puli and his/her larger cousin the Komondor are among my favorite dogs to watch in the Westminster and National dog shows! Others are the Great Dane and the schnauzers (giant, standard and mini, love them all).
@Spupilup there’s another conversation about this from earlier in the day. Both forms of the plural are acceptable in the M/W.

@CNB The only things I knew for sure in the lower third were BAATH and ACDC (I was pretty sure though I did flirt periodically with ASIA as I tried to make something work).  From there I figured BILL was likely, but not certain.  Same for ETHOS.    And I eventually plugged in HERO though I had not heard the quote.  After some more mucking around, I got INTHEAIR, SHORTS and BLOSSOM and TAB in place.  Then the real work began.  I eventually plugged in AFOUL and FAIR seemed likely.  I finally figured out PIN but even then I was stymied, trying desperately to come up with a woman’s name for 38 Down, and hoping a dog breed would occur to me.  I densely did not recognize CALLITINTHEAIR or DIGITALSHORTS as correct even when I had them in.  OLGA seemed likely based on the likely ethnicity of the last name, but that was a guess. Finally LILITH showed me the light.  So, to answer your question, by spending far too long on a crossword puzzle, my brain somehow finds a way.

Not sure what "purist" means here. Are you only interested in solving jigsaw puzzles that have been hand-cut? @Barry Ancona The term "purist" in my comment, means someone who does not rely on anything other than their own brain and what it might contain, to provide answers to a crossword's clues. No use of digital, internet, books, other people, etc. in coming up with the answers. I usually take a "purist" approach to the "Mini" and "Midi" puzzles here, but apply a different set of personal restrictions to the full crosswords, choosing to not invite complete frustration, but rather to just keep track of my "cheats" and the time it takes me to complete the puzzles as a measure of how well or poorly I've done. I'm only interested in comparing myself to myself.
Sorry if it wasn't clear. I understood what you meant for solvers. I was questioning what you meant for constructors and editors, and why it would matter.

I would add that since you have clarified that you are *not* a "purist" solving the "full" Crossword, I'm a bit surprised that you would comment on the supposed advantage of constructors who are not (whatever that would actually mean).
